title: Practice Set 7 — Monitoring and the Spark UI Deep Dive
description: Diagnose pipeline performance from Spark UI evidence: read stage-level metrics to find bottlenecks, detect task skew from duration distributions, identify GC pressure from executor metrics, recognize disk spill from stage tables, write monitoring queries against pipeline run history, and resolve a multi-symptom production slowdown.

datasets.description: Historical pipeline run metrics for 4 NordGrid pipelines over 30 days. 390 rows. Contains 3 injected anomalies.

tasks.title: Read Stage-Level Metrics to Find the Bottleneck

description.context: NordGrid's Gold billing pipeline took 11 minutes today instead of the usual 2 minutes. The Spark UI's Stages tab shows four stages.
description.task: Examine the stage metrics table below. Identify the bottleneck stage, determine what the shuffle and spill metrics indicate, and calculate the stage's percentage of total runtime.
description.validationHint: The bottleneck stage has the longest duration. Spill indicates memory pressure.

evidence.label: Spark UI — Stages tab for the Gold billing pipeline
evidence.content:
Stage | Description                   | Duration | Input    | Shuffle Read | Spill (Mem) | Spill (Disk) | Tasks
------|------------------------------|----------|----------|-------------|-------------|-------------|------
  0   | FileScan Silver readings      | 22s      | 3.4 GB   | —           | 0           | 0           | 24
  1   | FileScan meters_ref + Filter   | 0.8s     | 12 MB    | —           | 0           | 0           | 1
  2   | BroadcastHashJoin + Aggregate  | 9.8 min  | —        | 2.9 GB      | 8.1 GB      | 3.2 GB      | 200
  3   | Write Parquet                  | 4s       | 12 rows  | —           | 0           | 0           | 1

fields.label: Which stage is the bottleneck?
options: Stage 0 — the 3.4 GB file scan takes 22 seconds
Stage 2 — the join + aggregate takes 9.8 minutes with 3.2 GB disk spill
Stage 3 — the write produces only 12 rows which is suspicious
All stages contribute equally to the 11-minute runtime
fields.correctAnswer: Stage 2 — the join + aggregate takes 9.8 minutes with 3.2 GB disk spill

fields.label: What do the spill metrics (8.1 GB memory spill, 3.2 GB disk spill) indicate?
options: Normal behavior — all aggregations produce some spill
Memory pressure — the aggregation's intermediate data exceeds the executor memory budget, forcing Spark to write overflow data to disk
Disk failure — the executor's SSD cannot keep up
The spill is from Stage 0, not Stage 2
fields.correctAnswer: Memory pressure — the aggregation's intermediate data exceeds the executor memory budget, forcing Spark to write overflow data to disk

fields.label: What percentage of total pipeline runtime is Stage 2? (Total: 22s + 0.8s + 588s + 4s = 614.8s)

tasks.title: Detect Task Skew from Duration Distribution

description.context: Stage 2 of NordGrid's Gold billing pipeline has 200 tasks. The Spark UI's Summary Metrics show the task duration distribution.
description.task: Determine whether skew is present, identify the straggler, and explain the impact on stage duration.
description.validationHint: Compare max to median. A ratio above 10x strongly suggests skew.

taskDistribution.label: Stage 2 — Summary Metrics (200 tasks)
taskDistribution.content:
Metric          | Value
----------------|--------
Min duration    | 0.4s
Median          | 1.8s
P95             | 3.1s
Max duration    | 9.6 min

Total records: 1,200,000 | Median records/task: 5,200 | Max records: 498,000

fields.label: Is task skew present?
options: No — the distribution is normal
Yes — task 147 processes 498K records (96x the median of 5.2K) and takes 9.6 min (320x the median of 1.8s)
Inconclusive — need more data
Yes, but it's minor
fields.correctAnswer: Yes — task 147 processes 498K records (96x the median of 5.2K) and takes 9.6 min (320x the median of 1.8s)

fields.label: Task 147 has 2.9 GB disk spill and 45s GC time. What does this combination indicate?
options: The spill and GC are unrelated to the skew
The skewed partition exceeds per-task memory, causing spill. The large in-memory data triggers GC. All three symptoms stem from data skew.
The GC is from a memory leak
The spill is from the file scan
fields.correctAnswer: The skewed partition exceeds per-task memory, causing spill. The large in-memory data triggers GC. All three symptoms stem from data skew.

fields.label: Why does one straggler determine the entire stage duration?
options: Minimal impact — stages run in parallel
Spark must wait for all tasks to finish before the next stage. 199 tasks finish in under 4s, then the cluster idles 9.6 min waiting for task 147.
The straggler causes other tasks to fail
It only affects spill metrics, not wall-clock time
fields.correctAnswer: Spark must wait for all tasks to finish before the next stage. 199 tasks finish in under 4s, then the cluster idles 9.6 min waiting for task 147.

tasks.title: Diagnose GC Pressure from Executor Metrics

description.context: NordGrid's Gold billing pipeline is 3x slower than usual. The Executors tab shows unusual GC activity across all executors.
description.task: Identify the severity of GC pressure, determine the root cause, and recommend the configuration fix.
description.validationHint: GC% below 5% is healthy. Above 20% is severe.

fields.label: Is the GC pressure a problem?
options: No — GC at 30-35% is normal for data-intensive workloads
Yes — GC at 30-35% is severe. For every 3s of work, 1s is GC pause. This directly causes the 3x slowdown.
Inconclusive without knowing task types
No — GC is only on executors, not the driver
fields.correctAnswer: Yes — GC at 30-35% is severe. For every 3s of work, 1s is GC pause. This directly causes the 3x slowdown.

fields.label: What is the root cause?
options: The driver has too little memory
Executor memory (8 GB, ~6 GB usable) is insufficient for the per-executor data volume (~800 MB shuffle + spill). Hash tables exceed memory, triggering frequent full-GC.
JVM overhead is too high at 25%
Too many concurrent tasks (4 cores) competing for memory
fields.correctAnswer: Executor memory (8 GB, ~6 GB usable) is insufficient for the per-executor data volume (~800 MB shuffle + spill). Hash tables exceed memory, triggering frequent full-GC.

fields.label: What configuration change reduces GC pressure?
options: Increase driver memory to 8 GB
Increase spark.executor.memory from 8 GB to 16 GB
Decrease executors from 4 to 2
Increase shuffle partitions to 400
fields.correctAnswer: Increase spark.executor.memory from 8 GB to 16 GB

fields.label: 5 failed tasks (2+1+2+0) combined with high GC indicate what?
options: Random task failures
OutOfMemoryError during GC — JVM kills tasks when GC cannot reclaim enough memory. Failures correlate with highest-GC executors.
Network timeouts
Corrupt input data
fields.correctAnswer: OutOfMemoryError during GC — JVM kills tasks when GC cannot reclaim enough memory. Failures correlate with highest-GC executors.

tasks.title: Identify Disk Spill from Stage Metrics

description.context: NordGrid's Silver aggregation groups 1.2M readings by meter_id and month with shuffle.partitions=16. Stage metrics show significant spill.
description.task: Diagnose the spill, compute the target partition count, and identify alternative fixes.
description.validationHint: Spill happens when partition working set exceeds per-task memory.

evidence.label: Stage 1 (Exchange + HashAggregate)
evidence.content:
Duration: 6.2 min | Shuffle Read: 4.8 GB | Tasks: 16
Spill (Memory): 12.4 GB | Spill (Disk): 5.8 GB

Per-task: Avg shuffle read 300 MB | Avg spill 362 MB disk
Executor budget per task: ~1.5 GB (6 GB usable / 4 concurrent tasks)

The 300 MB input expands to ~1.8 GB hash table (14.4M groups per partition) exceeding the 1.5 GB budget.

fields.label: What is the difference between Spill (Memory) and Spill (Disk)?
options: Spill (Memory) is total data size; Spill (Disk) is compressed size on disk
Spill (Memory) is data that exceeded the memory budget for external merge. Spill (Disk) is the amount written to disk. Memory > Disk because disk version is compressed.
They should always be equal
Spill (Memory) is a warning; only Spill (Disk) matters
fields.correctAnswer: Spill (Memory) is data that exceeded the memory budget for external merge. Spill (Disk) is the amount written to disk. Memory > Disk because disk version is compressed.

fields.label: Target: each partition under 128 MB. How many shuffle partitions for 4.8 GB?
Besides increasing shuffle partitions, what alternatives reduce spill?
options: Pre-filter to reduce volume
Increase executor memory
Reduce cores per executor (more memory per task)
All three — best choice depends on constraints
fields.correctAnswer: All three — best choice depends on constraints

description.context: NordGrid logs every pipeline run to a metrics table. The team needs monitoring queries that detect: (1) duration spikes (>300s), (2) zero-row runs, and (3) spill events (spill_mb > 0).
description.task: Read PM7_data_pipeline_metrics.csv, register as a view, and write three queries. Store results in duration_spikes, zero_row_runs, spill_events.
description.validationHint: The dataset has 3 injected anomalies: 8-min join (day 15), 0-row failure (day 22), 5-min aggregation (day 28).

evidence.label: Pipeline metrics table schema
evidence.content: pipeline_id (string), run_date (string), stage_name (string), duration_s (double), rows_in (int), rows_out (int), shuffle_mb (int), spill_mb (int), status (string)

fields.label: Why check for zero-row runs when status = 'success'?
options: Spark marks zero-row runs as failed
A pipeline that reads/writes 0 rows completes successfully — no error is raised. Only monitoring catches the silent failure.
Zero rows indicates a network partition
Zero-row runs are impossible
fields.correctAnswer: A pipeline that reads/writes 0 rows completes successfully — no error is raised. Only monitoring catches the silent failure.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-monitoring-queries') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .getOrCreate()

df_metrics = spark.read.csv('PM7_data_pipeline_metrics.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
df_metrics.createOrReplaceTempView('pipeline_metrics')

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# 1. duration_spikes: stages where duration_s > 300
# 2. zero_row_runs: (pipeline_id, run_date) where any stage has rows_in = 0
# 3. spill_events: rows where spill_mb > 0



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
print(f"Duration spikes: {duration_spikes.count()}")
duration_spikes.show()
print(f"Zero-row runs: {zero_row_runs.count()}")
zero_row_runs.show()
print(f"Spill events: {spill_events.count()}")
spill_events.show()

assertions.check: isinstance(duration_spikes, DataFrame) and isinstance(zero_row_runs, DataFrame) and isinstance(spill_events, DataFrame)

description.context: NordGrid's Gold billing pipeline took 14 minutes instead of 2. Evidence from three Spark UI tabs is provided. Build the diagnosis chain across all three views.
description.task: Identify the bottleneck stage, check for task skew, check executor health, determine root cause, and write the config fix.
description.validationHint: One skewed partition causes one straggler task, which causes spill and GC on one executor.

stageMetrics.label: Stages tab
stageMetrics.content: Stage 0: FileScan 25s | Stage 1: Broadcast 0.3s | Stage 2: Join+Agg 13.2 min, Spill 2.8 GB, 200 tasks | Stage 3: Write 3s

taskMetrics.label: Stage 2 — Task distribution
taskMetrics.content:
Median: 2.1s | P95: 3.4s | Max: 12.8 min (Task 147)
Task 147: 502K records (vs median 5.1K), 520 MB shuffle read, 2.8 GB spill, 52s GC

executorMetrics.label: Executors tab
executorMetrics.content:
Executor 1: GC 7.3%, 790s task time — processed the straggler
Executors 2-4: GC 4-6%, 48-55s task time — normal

fields.label: What is the bottleneck?
options: Stage 0 — the file scan
Stage 2 — join + aggregate at 13.2 min with 2.8 GB spill
Stage 3 — the write
All stages equally
fields.correctAnswer: Stage 2 — join + aggregate at 13.2 min with 2.8 GB spill

fields.label: Is the bottleneck from overall volume or task skew?
options: Overall volume — 3.1 GB is too much
Task skew — task 147 has 502K records (98x median) taking 12.8 min while 199 tasks finish in <4s. Spill is concentrated on task 147.
Both equally
Neither — it's GC pressure across all executors
fields.correctAnswer: Task skew — task 147 has 502K records (98x median) taking 12.8 min while 199 tasks finish in <4s. Spill is concentrated on task 147.

fields.label: Why does executor 1 have higher GC (7.3%) than executors 2-4 (4-6%)?
options: Hardware problem on executor 1
Executor 1 processed the skewed task 147 — its 502K records caused memory pressure and elevated GC. Other executors processed normal tasks.
All executors have the same GC — normal variance
Executor 1 has less memory
fields.correctAnswer: Executor 1 processed the skewed task 147 — its 502K records caused memory pressure and elevated GC. Other executors processed normal tasks.

fields.label: Complete diagnosis chain?
options: Insufficient memory -> GC -> failures -> slowdown
Skewed key -> one partition gets 42% of data -> straggler task 147 -> spill -> GC on executor 1 -> 199 tasks wait 12.8 min -> 14 min total
Small files -> slow scan -> cascade
Wrong join strategy -> shuffle -> spill -> GC
fields.correctAnswer: Skewed key -> one partition gets 42% of data -> straggler task 147 -> spill -> GC on executor 1 -> 199 tasks wait 12.8 min -> 14 min total

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-slowdown-fix') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .getOrCreate()

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# Fix the skewed aggregation with config changes.
# Option 1: Increase shuffle partitions to spread skewed key
# Option 2: Enable AQE for automatic skew handling
# Apply configs to spark session.



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
print(f"shuffle.partitions: {spark.conf.get('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions')}")
try:
    print(f"AQE enabled: {spark.conf.get('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled')}")
except:
    print('AQE config not set')

assertions.description: Config changed: higher shuffle partitions or AQE enabled

conceptsCombined: Task skew detection from duration distribution (Lesson 3-4)
GC pressure detection from Executors tab (Lesson 5-6)
synthesisNotes.rationale: The synthesis requires correlating evidence across three Spark UI views to build a causal chain from root cause to symptom. This three-view correlation is the core skill of Spark UI diagnosis.

selfCheck.evidenceRealistic: Stage metrics, task distributions, and executor tables modeled on Spark 3.5+ UI output.
selfCheck.theoryPracticeBoundary: Theory teaches individual UI tabs. Practice requires cross-tab correlation for incident diagnosis.
